title: St. Barkisos, St. Mercurius, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Methodius of Olympus
narrative:
title": "St. Barkisos, St. Mercurius, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Methodius of Olympus

narrative": "title\": \"St. Barkisos, St. Mercurius, St. Alexander of Alexandria & St. Methodius of Olympus

narrative\": \"St. Barkisos of Persia, St. Mercurius the Great Martyr, St. Alexander of Alexandria, and St. Methodius of Olympus

On the first day of Megabit, the holy Church opens the month with four witnesses whose lives are bound together by the great Lenten themes of combat, confession, and the costliness of truth. Saint Barkisos is a martyr of the Persian church, one of the great company of bishops, priests, and faithful who perished under the persecutions of Shapur II, when Christianity's association with the Roman Empire made its adherents suspect in Persian eyes and waves of execution swept through the eastern church with terrible regularity. Barkisos faced his interrogators with the calm that the Synaxarium tradition reserves for those who have so thoroughly prepared for martyrdom in the life of prayer that the hour of testing comes as a consummation rather than a catastrophe. He is honoured as a father of the Persian church and a witness to the universal reach of the Faith.

Saint Mercurius, called the Great Martyr, is one of the most celebrated soldier-saints of the Oriental Christian world — a Scythian officer in the Roman army who was visited by the Archangel Michael before battle, promised victory, and subsequently commanded to confess Christ. After leading his troops to the victory the angel had foretold, Mercurius refused the honours that Decius wished to press upon him and instead declared his faith, for which he was tortured with extreme ingenuity and finally beheaded. The Ethiopian tradition particularly venerates Mercurius because the later tradition associates him with an intercessory apparition in which he appeared armed, by divine permission, to protect the Church from a particular earthly threat — making him in the Ethiopian devotional imagination a living intercessor whose power did not cease at his martyrdom.

Saint Alexander of Alexandria is the great patriarch whose theological courage made the Council of Nicaea possible: it was Alexander who first identified the Arian position as incompatible with the tradition, who called the local synod that condemned Arius, and who arrived at Nicaea with his deacon Athanasius already at his side, already having fought the battle that the council was assembled to settle. His contribution to Nicaea is often overshadowed by Athanasius's subsequent decades of struggle, but Alexander was the one who understood first, framed the question correctly, and ensured that the council did not settle for a compromise that would have left the Arian infection festering in the body of the Church.

Saint Methodius of Olympus is this day's figure of intellectual discovery: a bishop and theologian of Asia Minor who wrote before Nicaea, in the late third and early fourth centuries, and whose works — dialogues modelled formally on Plato but filled with Christian content — represent the most sophisticated engagement of early Christian thought with classical philosophical forms before the Cappadocians. His Symposium or Banquet of the Ten Virgins reimagines the Platonic symposium as a Christian discourse on virginity and the soul's orientation toward God, and his writings on the resurrection of the body directly challenged the Origenist allegorisation of that doctrine in ways that anticipated the Nicene debates about the full reality of Christ's humanity. That he was eventually martyred under Diocletian gives his intellectual witness the seal of blood. Amen.
